title: Netskope DLP Policy Violations and Sensitive Data Exfiltration Attempts
spl:
index=casb sourcetype="netskope:events" earliest=-7d
| eval dlp=coalesce(dlp_profile, dlp_rule, policy_name)
| eval sev=lower(coalesce(severity, dlp_severity, "medium"))
| where match(lower(coalesce(activity, ni_activity, "")),"(?i)upload|post|share|create|edit") AND match(lower(dlp),"(?i)dlp|confidential|pci|pii|secret")
| stats count by user, app_name, dlp, action, sev
| sort -count
| head 40
description: Netskope DLP identifies regulated data movement toward unsanctioned SaaS instances, personal webmail, or generative AI tools. Tracking violations by rule profile and enforcement action proves control effectiveness for privacy and sector regulators.
value: Data protection teams leverage Netskope's unique instance detection to distinguish DLP violations to corporate vs personal cloud app instances, prioritizing exfiltration to personal instances as high-risk insider threat signals requiring immediate investigation.
implementation: Ensure DLP dictionaries and exact data match fields are enabled in tenant policy. Mask or hash sensitive literals at ingestion if required by legal. Route critical severities to a restricted index with stricter RBAC. Quarterly review false positives with data owners.
visualization: Table (user, application, DLP profile), Heatmap (severity × action), Sankey (app → destination domain).
knownFalsePositives: DLP hits from authorized data exports, finance reports, contracts with PII content sent to vendors.
grandmaExplanation: Netskope can tell the difference between someone uploading sensitive data to the company's cloud storage versus their personal account. Uploading to a personal account is a much bigger deal — it means the data is leaving the company — so those incidents get investigated immediately.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Netskope DLP events in `index=casb` with `sourcetype=netskope:events`. Key DLP fields: `dlp_profile` (PCI/HIPAA/PII/Custom), `dlp_rule`, `dlp_severity` (info/low/medium/high/critical), `dlp_action` (allow/block/alert/user_alert), `dlp_incident_id`, `file_name`, `file_type`, `file_size`, `user`, `app_name`, `activity` (upload/download/share/edit/post), `instance_name` (corporate vs personal instance).
• Netskope DLP inspects content inline in cloud apps and web traffic. Key differentiator: it can distinguish between corporate and personal instances of the same app (e.g., corporate OneDrive vs personal OneDrive). DLP violations to personal instances are high-risk exfiltration signals.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify DLP events:
```spl
index=casb sourcetype="netskope:events" earliest=-7d
| where isnotnull(dlp_profile)
| stats count by dlp_profile, dlp_action, activity
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — DLP violation analysis:
```spl
index=casb sourcetype="netskope:events" earliest=-7d
| where isnotnull(dlp_profile)
| stats count dc(user) as users by dlp_profile, dlp_action, app_name, instance_name
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL: Groups DLP violations by profile, enforcement action, and app instance. The `instance_name` field is critical: violations to 'Personal' instances of cloud apps indicate deliberate exfiltration — an employee uploading PCI data to their personal Google Drive is fundamentally different from uploading to the corporate instance.

Personal instance exfiltration:
```spl
index=casb sourcetype="netskope:events" earliest=-7d
| where isnotnull(dlp_profile) AND match(instance_name, "(?i)personal")
| stats count sum(file_size) as total_bytes by user, app_name, dlp_profile, activity
| eval total_mb=round(total_bytes/1048576, 1)
| sort - total_mb
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Upload a test file with PCI test data to a personal cloud instance. Verify DLP detection.
(b) In Netskope: Incidents > DLP. Compare with Splunk.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — DLP by profile and action. Row 2 — Personal instance violations. Row 3 — Top users.
Alert: Immediate on DLP violations to personal instances with `dlp_severity >= high`.

Runbook:
1. **Personal instance DLP** — High-risk exfiltration. Investigate user intent. Escalate to insider risk.
2. **Corporate instance DLP** — May be workflow issue. Review with data owner.
3. **Allow on high-severity DLP** — Policy gap. Update to block.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No DLP events** — DLP profiles may not be enabled in the Netskope policy.
• **`instance_name` empty** — Instance detection may not be configured for all apps.
